definition: CMS is bound to producing 508 compliant applications. Section 508 refers to accessibility requirements for federal electronic and information technology.
A specific list of drugs. A plan may have several tiers, and the copayment amount depends on which tier a drug is listed.
A/R Days, or Accounts Receivable Days, measures how long it takes a healthcare organization to collect payment after services are billed.
The collection of information from the medical record by hard copy or electronic instrument.
Improper behaviors or billing practices, including billing for non-covered services, misusing claim codes, or inappropriately allocating costs on a cost report.
When another person does something on purpose that causes mental or physical harm or pain.
A person's ability to get needed medical care and services.
A person's ability to get medical care and services when needed.
A separate housing arrangement within a single-family home that includes a complete living unit with a private kitchen and bath.
An evaluative process in which a healthcare organization is examined by an external accrediting body to determine whether it meets defined criteria.
The duration of CMS recognition of the validity of an accrediting organization's determination that a Medicare+Choice organization is fully accredited.
Some states use findings of private accreditation organizations to supplement or substitute for state oversight of certain quality-related standards.
A state requirement that plans must be accredited to participate in the Medicaid managed care program.
Having a seal of approval; a facility or healthcare organization has met quality standards set by nationally recognized groups.
An organization accredited by ANSI for the development of American National Standards.
Legislation that passed through Congress and was signed by the President or passed over a veto.
Activities usually done during a normal day, such as getting in and out of bed, dressing, bathing, eating, and using the bathroom.
The amount a doctor or supplier charges for a medical service or supply. It is often more than the Medicare-approved amount.
The difference between the summarized income rate and summarized cost rate over a specified valuation period.
A negative actuarial balance.
One half of the expected monthly SMI program cost for aged or disabled enrollees for the period the rate is in effect.
A measure of the adequacy of Hospital Insurance and Supplementary Medical Insurance financing based on the difference between trust fund assets and liabilities for specified periods.
A measure of financing adequacy based on the difference between assets and liabilities at the end of the periods for which financing was established.
Health care services not covered by Medicare, or reductions in premiums or cost sharing for Medicare-covered services, offered by a Medicare Advantage organization.
An estimate of how much Medicare will spend in a year for an average beneficiary.
How premium rates are decided based on members' use of benefits rather than individual use of benefits.
Code sets that characterize a general business situation rather than a medical condition or service. Under HIPAA these are sometimes called non-clinical or non-medical code sets.
A general term referring to Medicare, Medicaid, and CMS administrative costs, including program administration expenses.
Information collected, processed, and stored in automated information systems, including enrollment, eligibility, claims, and managed care encounters.
Expenses incurred by HHS and the Treasury in administering the SMI program and related contribution collection provisions.
A hearings officer who presides over appeal conflicts between providers or beneficiaries and Medicare contractors.
An arrangement where a self-insured entity contracts with a third-party administrator to administer a health plan.
The HIPAA provisions authorizing HHS to adopt standards for transactions, code sets, standard identifiers, and privacy/security protections.
A 2001 law that extended HIPAA electronic and code set transaction compliance timing and addressed payment of paper claims under certain conditions.
The Administrator of the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services.
The date the patient was admitted for inpatient care, outpatient service, start of care, or hospice election effective date.
A code indicating the patient's diagnosis at admission.
The doctor responsible for admitting a patient to a hospital or other inpatient health facility.
A residential care facility for beneficiaries who cannot live alone but do not need around-the-clock skilled medical services.
A notice a doctor or supplier should give when Medicare is expected to deny payment for an item or service. If properly given and signed, the beneficiary may be responsible for payment.
A decision made by a Private Fee-for-Service Plan about whether it will pay for a certain service.
A written document prepared ahead of time that states how medical decisions should be made if a person loses the ability to make decisions.
Written documents stating how medical decisions should be made if a person loses decision-making ability. They may include a Living Will and Durable Power of Attorney for health care.
A council historically appointed to study and review the financial status of the OASDI and Medicare programs.
A person who gives support or protects a person's rights.
A Medicare carrier, fiscal intermediary, or other contractor that shares some or all of a program safeguard contractor's jurisdiction.
A health care provider or facility that is paid by a health plan to provide services to plan members.
An individual age 65 or older who is enrolled in the SMI program.
A simple protein in the blood; its level may reflect protein intake from food.
A rule or procedure with conditional logic for solving a problem or completing a task; in health care it can support guideline or criteria evaluation.
The charge determined by a carrier for a covered SMI medical service or supply.
CDI stands for Clinical Documentation Integrity. It focuses on improving the accuracy, completeness, and clarity of clinical documentation.
Charge capture is the process of recording the services, procedures, supplies, medications, or tests provided to a patient so they can be billed.
Clean Claim Rate measures the percentage of claims submitted correctly the first time without needing correction, rework, or additional information.
A clinical workflow is the sequence of steps clinicians and staff follow to deliver care, document information, and coordinate patient activities.
Contract compliance measures whether purchases are being made according to negotiated supplier contracts, pricing, and terms.
Cutover is the planned transition from an old system or process to a new system or process.
Data conversion is the process of transforming data from one systemâ€™s format into the structure required by another system.
Data mapping defines how fields in one system correspond to fields in another system.
Denial Rate is the percentage of claims denied by payers after submission.
DNFB stands for Discharged Not Final Billed. It refers to accounts for patients who have been discharged but whose final bill has not yet been sent.
An EHR, or Electronic Health Record, is the digital system used to document, manage, and access patient clinical information.
FTE stands for Full-Time Equivalent. It is a way to measure staffing capacity based on full-time work hours.
HCAHPS is a standardized survey that measures patientsâ€™ perspectives on hospital care and patient experience.
A hospital-acquired condition is a complication or adverse condition that develops during a hospital stay and may have been preventable.
The item master is the central list of supplies, products, equipment, and materials used by an organization, including item descriptions, units of measure, pricing, and vendor information.
A legacy system is an older technology platform that an organization still uses, often because it supports critical business processes.
Overtime Rate measures how much labor is being worked beyond standard scheduled hours.
PAR level is the target quantity of an item that should be kept on hand in a supply location.
Patient access refers to how easily patients can schedule, reach, and receive care from providers or services.
Payer mix describes the proportion of patients or revenue coming from different payer types, such as Medicare, Medicaid, commercial insurance, self-pay, or managed care.
A preference card lists the supplies, instruments, equipment, and setup preferences needed for a specific procedure or provider.
Provider productivity measures the volume or value of clinical work performed by a physician or advanced practice provider.
Readmission Rate measures how often patients return to the hospital within a certain period after discharge, commonly within 30 days.
A stockout occurs when a needed item is unavailable when and where it is required.
UAT stands for User Acceptance Testing. It is the phase where end users test whether a system supports real business processes before go-live.
Vacancy Rate measures the percentage of approved positions that are currently unfilled.
Value-based care is a healthcare payment and delivery model that rewards quality, outcomes, efficiency, and patient experience rather than only volume of services.
wRVU stands for Work Relative Value Unit. It is used to measure the relative work effort associated with clinical services.
